coredata.dc:title: Period doubling and route to chaos in reduced graphene oxide, an experimental evidence
coredata.dc:creator: Faraji, Mohammad
Sharif, Morteza A.
Borjkhani, Mehdi
Ashabi, K.
coredata.dc:description: We use Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE) to investigate chaotic dynamics inside a graphene-based optical system. Numerical results show that with increasing the control parameters, the character of Modulation instability (MI) is changed from convective to absolute. In consequence, a hyper-chaotic map is more expressive to explain the dynamical state of the system. It is also indicated that a reverse transition from the chaotic regime to the stable state can be resulted if the optical input intensity is further increased. The optical input intensity and feedback strength are distinguished as the two control parameters of the system. We then use Reduced Graphene Oxide (RGO) dispersion with large nonlinear refractive index to verify if a transition to instability and chaotic behavior is experimentally realized for the visible input light wave. The observation begins with saturation in the nonlinear response followed by an Optical Bistability (OB) phenomena. Afterward, a quasi-periodic fluctuations appear expressing the absolute MI. In agreement with our simulation result based on the period doubling bifurcation diagram, this quasi-periodic state is recognized as a route to the chaos. We infer that the graphene Quantum Dots (QDs) which are formed as a result of reduction process are responsible for energy transfer through the optical near-field interactions and thus, strengthen the required feedback.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical bistability
Nonlinear Schrödinger equation
Chaos
Modulation instability
Reduced graphene oxide

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been funded and supported by Islamic Azad University , Parand branch, Parand, Tehran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Time evolution of amplitude for unstable wave propagation, (b) bifurcation diagram, (c) chaotic attractor for feedback strength ρ = 0.2.
(a) Time evolution of amplitude for unstable wave propagation, (b) chaotic attractor, (c) phase portrait for feedback strength ρ = 0.8.
(a) UV–Vis spectrum of GO and RGO dispersion, (b) its Tauc plot (Red dashed line: tangential line).
CA Z-scan diagram for three different concentrations of RGO sample.
Experimental data (square points) vs. the fitted bifurcation diargram (solid curve).
(a) Time evolution of amplitude, (b) Lyapunov exponents vs. input powers.

head.ce:title: Period doubling and route to chaos in reduced graphene oxide, an experimental evidence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer and IT Engineering, Parand Branch, Islamic Azad University, 3761396361 Parand, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Computer and IT Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Optics & Laser Engineering Group, Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Urmia University of Technology, Band road, 57155-419 Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MEAPAC Service, L.L.C. One Research Court Suite 450, Rockville, MD 2850, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We use Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE) to investigate chaotic dynamics inside a graphene-based optical system. Numerical results show that with increasing the control parameters, the character of Modulation instability (MI) is changed from convective to absolute. In consequence, a hyper-chaotic map is more expressive to explain the dynamical state of the system. It is also indicated that a reverse transition from the chaotic regime to the stable state can be resulted if the optical input intensity is further increased. The optical input intensity and feedback strength are distinguished as the two control parameters of the system. We then use Reduced Graphene Oxide (RGO) dispersion with large nonlinear refractive index to verify if a transition to instability and chaotic behavior is experimentally realized for the visible input light wave. The observation begins with saturation in the nonlinear response followed by an Optical Bistability (OB) phenomena. Afterward, a quasi-periodic fluctuations appear expressing the absolute MI. In agreement with our simulation result based on the period doubling bifurcation diagram, this quasi-periodic state is recognized as a route to the chaos. We infer that the graphene Quantum Dots (QDs) which are formed as a result of reduction process are responsible for energy transfer through the optical near-field interactions and thus, strengthen the required feedback.

ce:section.ce:para: Chaotic wave propagation has been contemplated since the 90th decade as a security agent for optical communication. Developing toward the ultrafast pulsed lasers, the need to control the chaos has been seriously taken into account. B. R. Andrievskii, et al. have declared methods and applications of controlling chaos through their review article [1]. L. Illing, et al. have presented a full study of controlling the optical chaos. They have explained that the optical chaos communication can be achieved by electro-optical feedback devices [2].Today, the investigation on chaotic dynamics is expanded to the novel fields of optics and photonics for which the stability considerations seem to be crucial. Chaos is a characteristic feature of the nonlinear optical systems using a feedback mechanism. The latter can be provided by a resonating cavity (e.g. a Fabry-Perot cavity) in which the nonlinear medium is laid. Accordingly, a high power optical beam can interact with the nonlinearity; if the feedback wave is retarded enough to be formed such that the delay time becomes longer than the nonlinear medium response time, the interaction will undergo a complex chaotic procedure [3,4]. The route to chaos may be different from one optical system to another. Previously, it has been shown that the Ikeda instability is a proper chaotic map to describe the dynamical state of an unstable optical system [5–8]. However, it is now revealed that a hyper-chaotic procedure can interfere in. On the other hand, several literature contexts have addressed the effect of Modulation Instability (MI) as a prior state to chaotic regime in a typical nonlinear optical system. MI occurs when the balance between the nonlinearity and dispersion/diffraction is violated. The consequence is the occurrence of a quasi-periodic state. For certain criterion, the character of MI changes from convective to absolute. In this case, the amplitude of the quasi-periodic state will grow fast. Subsequently, an unpredictable state will appear in which tracing the nonlinear dynamics will reveal a chaotic behavior. [8–11]. Before beginning the MI procedure, depending on the feedback strength, the system can either experience a saturation in nonlinearity, known as optical limiting or jump to upper stable states recognized as Optical Bistability (OB) and multistability.

Nanostructure based optical systems exhibit extremely large nonlinearity and can serve as suitable nonlinear media for different applications like ultrashort pulse generation, optical modulation, plasmonics, etc. [12–25]. Herein, the propagation of optical wave is expected to be unstable even if a low input power is illuminated to the nanostructure based nonlinear media; a challenge that intensely influences on the device performance through a desired application. A set of studies have theoretically investigated and explained the circumstances of OB and multistability in nanostructure based optical systems [26–31]. In contrast, a few number of papers have experimentally reported the observation of OB and multistabilty [32–35]. Meanwhile, the investigation of chaotic dynamical state is only limited to the theoretical studies with consideration of sub-atomic interactions and quantum coherence [36]; few of them have dealt with the nonlinear medium related measurable/macroscopic quantities required for evaluating an experimental report.

Graphene is a 2D nanostructure that exhibits strong and fast optical nonlinear response [35,37–43]. On this base, graphene - based optical devices and systems have been proposed for nonlinear optical applications [12–15,18,20]. Through the previous article, OB and multi-stability have been experimentally verified in few layer graphene oxide ink for the low threshold optical power at a visible frequency. It has been theoretically predicted that the nonlinearity can be enhanced for terahertz frequency range inasmuch as the output wave can be unstable and chaotic [35]. In this study, we show that period doubling and a transition to chaos can be experimentally occurred for a visible light inside a medium containing few layer dispersed Reduced Graphene Oxide (RGO). We theoretically explore the procedure of route to chaos in accordance with nonlinear optical characteristics of the medium.

Third order nonlinear optical conductivity of single layer graphene can be written as Eq.(1) for interband transitions if the Third Harmonic Generation (THG) is generally assumed as the prevailing nonlinear phenomena [38,39,41].
(1)

ω 2
ω 3

,

F is the Fermi velocity and ω
t = ω 1 + ω 2 + ω 3. The third order nonlinear susceptibility can be given by Eq. (2) [38,39,41].
(2)

where ε 0 is the free space permittivity and d
gr is the thickness of single layer graphene. An estimation of the nonlinear refractive index n 2 can be obtained by Eq. (3).
(3)

in which c is the free space light velocity and χ (1) = σ (1)/(−iωε 0 d
gr ) is the linear susceptibility in terms of surface conductivity σ (1) given in Eq. (4).
(4)

,
μ is the chemical potential of graphene; k
B is the Boltzmann's constant; T denotes the temperature and τ is known as relaxation time [38,39,41].

RGO is an alternative to the pristine graphene for its similar optical and electrical characteristics. However, the preparation of RGO is easier compared to that of graphene considering the simple and low-cost preparation process for GO and the availability of multifarious reduction methods [42–50]. On the side, the degree of reduction determines the optical and electrical characteristics. This will provide a wide range of selection for optical transparency, band gap and nonlinearity [50–53].

If a single layer graphene is suspended inside a dielectric medium, the nonlinear response due to THG will be resonated for certain thicknesses of dielectric slab d given in Eq. (5).
(5)
d =
λ / 2
ε
m ,
m = 0 , 1 , 2 , …
in which λ is the light wavelength and ε is the dielectric permittivity. If this nonlinear system is supported on a metal thin film, m should be replaced with m + 1/2 in Eq. (5) and a giant enhancement of nonlinear response will be resulted for resonating modes [42]. This implies the increasing in the nonlinear optical conductivity and subsequently the effective nonlinear refractive index.

Eq. (5) is also valid for a suspended RGO sheet in accordance with Section 2.2. If otherwise, it is assumed that multiple RGO flakes are embedded inside a dielectric medium, the nonlinearity enhancement will be further increased. This is an experimental verification [32,35] which will be shown in the experimental section by increasing the RGO suspension concentration.

Using the density matrix formalism, Makoto Naruse et al. have shown that for a nanoscale optical system containing a pair of Quantum Dots (QDs), chaotic oscillation can occur as a result of energy transfer through the optical near-field interactions [36]. When a time delay incorporates between two energy transfers within the nonlinear system, optical pulses will appear even if the system is pumped by a cw radiation. Consequently, QDs are refereed as optical nano-pulsers. These optical pulse trains can grow fast in amplitude if the QDs' pairs are strongly coupled. Solving for nano-pulsers' population, chaotic oscillatory behavior will be obtained for certain criterion [36].

Indeed, the latter mentioned nanoscale optical system stands for a more inclusive concept: the spaser; its nonlinear mechanism is analogous to that of a laser cavity for which the mirrors and coherently generated photons are respectively substituted with the couples of QDs and localized surface plasmon polaritons. In the presence of thousands of QDs' pairs coupled with near-field interactions, surface plasmon polaritons can be intensely amplified. Thereupon, the ultrafast quasi-periodic behavior may be complex and unpredictable and a transition to chaos will be possible. For an exact nonlinear analysis, it is essential to estimate the plasmon resonance modes. This will need information about the compositional/peripheral structure of the nanoscale optical system including the nanoparticles' size, shapes, dielectric function, etc. [54]. Notwithstanding, evolution of instability in a spaser is still an open topic for researchers.

In this study, in order to investigate the nonlinear dynamics inside a mesocopic optical system containing RGO dispersion, we use an alternative formalism i.e. the Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (NLSE) which is given in Eq. (6).
(6)
∂ U
∂ z
+
1 2
ik 2
∂ 2
U
∂
t 2
= iγ
U
2
U ,

U is the slowly varying complex amplitude; t and z determine the time and light propagation direction respectively; γ = 2nε 0 n 2 ω is the nonlinearity whereas n is the refractive index; k 2 = d/dω(1/v
g (ω)) ω=ω 0 in which ω is the angular frequency, ω 0 denotes the free space frequency and v
g is the group velocity [55]. If we seeks unstable solutions, we should numerically solve NLSE by a Finite Difference based method like Predictor- Corrector Method (PCM) [56] from which the properties of nonlinearity and other known characteristics are guaranteed to be preserved through the optical system due to its strict dependence on the computational grid points distributed along the propagation length. This in turn, provides an adaptive step-size control strategy [56]. The criterion under which the solution will be unstable and the quasi-period state may experience a transition toward chaos, is given in Eq. (7) [11].
(7)
γ
U 0
2
>
v g
k 2
2
+
ω
v g
,
which means that the multiplication of optical input intensity and nonlinearity must be larger than a threshold value. Here, we introduce the feedback strength ρ as another determinative control parameter which can be measured by multiplying the mirrors' reflectivities within the structure of an interferometer used in the experimental setup. The quantity lies between 0 and 1 and indicates the percentage of effective reflectance for the optical wave appeared as the feedback wave.

As mentioned, we assume a nonlinear medium containing RGO ink enclosed within a Fabry-Perot resonator. We use the PCM [56] to simulate the nonlinear dynamics inside the nonlinear medium. Eqs. (1)–(3) are used to yield the single layer graphene nonlinear refractive index. The initial values of the required parameters including the light wave input intensity and wavelength are set in accordance with our experimental consideration given in the next section. Afterward, PCM will modify the nonlinearity along the propagation axis based on the number of suspended graphene layers. During the simulation process, the input intensity is gradually raised inasmuch as the criterion of Eq. (7) is finally satisfied. Hereafter, the nonlinear dynamics of instability can be analyzed. The numerical results are intensely sensitive to the input intensity and feedback strength provided by Fabry-Perot resonator as given in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 .

The alteration of the system state is clearly observable through the bifurcation diagram (Fig. 1(b)); a sudden jump from the lower stable state to the upper one at 1 × 104 W/m2 implies the occurrence of OB. Beyond the threshold intensity (about 2 × 104 W/m2), the criterion of Eq. (7) is fulfilled and period doubling cascades appear. Consequently, if the input intensity is further increased, a quasi-periodic state (with growing amplitude) inferred as absolute MI will be dominant; thereafter the temporal behavior will be more complex and unpredictable as shown in Fig. 1(a). Nevertheless, a reverse procedure at the input intensity of about 6 × 104 W/m2 appears through which the chaotic behavior transforms toward stability. Meanwhile, Fig.1(c) - as the chaotic attractor diagram- denotes a hyper-chaotic procedure.

If otherwise the feedback strength is increased up to 0.8 for a lower input intensity compared to that of assumed for Fig.1 (a), the feature of amplification will be intensified as we already expect (Fig. 2(a) as the time evolution of the amplitude). However there is still a concordant multi-periodic state recognized as convective MI. Fig.1 (b) and Fig.1 (c) show the attractor diagram and phase portrait respectively.

We use an as-prepared dispersed few layer GO in Dimethylformamide (DMF) prepared by exfoliation method [57]. We then reduce GO by Hydrazine for 1 h at 80 °C. We choose DMF for the reason of affording high stability for RGO suspension. An observable change in dispersion color from dark yellow to soft black implies the chemical transformation from GO to RGO. We use a Perkin Elmer UV–Vis NIR Lambda 950 spectrophotometer to obtain UV–Vis spectrum of RGO dispersion as given in Fig. 3 . It is clear that the GO absorbance is lower than that of RGO for a wide range of wavelengths and the optical transparency is thus higher. On the other hand, from the Tauc plot (Fig. 3(b)), one can deduce that the optical band gap of RGO is lower than that of GO.

We use Close Aperture (CA) Z-scan technique to measure the nonlinear refractive index of the medium containing dispersed RGO sheets. This is done by illuminating a focused laser beam to the nonlinear medium through an aperture and scanning along the optical axis [58]. A 532.8 nm Nd-YAG laser with TE polarization is used as the light source. Three different concentrations of RGO dispersion are considered. Fig. 4 indicates the CA Z-scan data and Table 1 categorizes the measured nonlinear refractive indices for each concentration.

The large measured value for the nonlinear refractive index of RGO is consistent with the experimental reports and can be reasoned by the chemical and electrical similarities to the pristine graphene which will also bring the optical resemblance. A stronger nonlinear response can be obtained by tuning the size of sp 2 domains resulting from the reduction process [59]. This will particularly include the optical limiting and the nonlinearity saturation as we will experimentally show in the next section.

As the next step, using optical attenuator we gradually increase/decrease the input power of the optical beam illuminated to a 3 mm quartz cell containing RGO ink. We take the highest concentration given in Table 1 in order to get the largest nonlinear response. The nonlinear medium is included in a Fabry-Perot resonator. The feedback strength in our experiment is estimated to be about 0.1. A power meter (Pooyafar Azma 0–500 mW) is used to record the output signal from the nonlinear medium. Fig. 5 shows the experimental result.

In fact, Fig.5 includes an abrupt growth in the amplitude followed by a saturation in the nonlinear response. In addition, the hysteresis loop indicates an unusual OB behavior compared to the previously reported experimental observation of OB in GO ink [35]. The effect is interpreted such that the instability begins but does not fully proceed. In lieu, a return to stable state is fulfilled.

Through the simulation stage, we cannot obtain a hysteresis loop similar to that given in Fig. 5 if we only look for stable solutions of NLSE (Eq. (6)). Therefore, such behavior is a distinctive feature denoting the probable proximity to instability.

As the next step, we increase the input power further to investigate if the theoretical prediction about the transition to chaos is realized. The experimental data are presented via square points in Fig. 6 . Accordingly, we guess a transition to complex behavior commencing beyond the optical input power of 80 mW.

Using the measured nonlinear refractive indices given in Table 1 and assuming that ρ = 0.1, we repeat the simulation method given in Section 3 to obtain a corresponding bifurcation diagram. The result is shown through the solid green curve in Fig. 6 which is in agreement with experimental observation of OB (Fig. 5) and our conjecture on transition to chaos.

We are also interested in the temporal behavior in a similar manner to that given in Fig. 1(a) or Fig. 2(a). We use a HAMAMATSU Si APD photodiode (S5345) as photodetector and a RIGOL oscilloscope (DS2302A) as output signal recorder. For the optical input power of 100 mW, the data is recorded as shown in the inset of Fig. 6 in which a period-doubling behavior (the universal formalism of transition to chaos) is clearly observable.

As well, we increase the input power up to 300 mW to observe if the time evolution of the output wave is influenced or not. The result is shown in Fig. 7(a). Hereof, multi-frequency quasi-periodic state with a fast growing rate of the amplitude appears. This in turn, implies the absolute MI procedure. Besides, we compare the dynamical behavior between the two cases with 100 mW and 300 mW input power by determining the Lyapunov exponent as shown in Fig. 7(b). For both cases, the Lyapunov exponents are positive values which indicate exponential growing rate of the amplitude. Nonetheless, the value is larger for 300 mW input power (0.48) in comparison to the case of 100 mW input power (0.03). This is in complete agreement with Eq. (7) which predicts that the instability grows faster for the larger input powers.

Reduction process of GO will modify the electronic structure such that graphene QDs will be formed within the RGO sheets [52]. As aforementioned in Section 2.4, these QDs have the role of nano-pulsers and thus can transfer energy through the optical near-field interactions [36]. This means the higher feedback strength providing the appropriate condition for changing the character of MI from convective to absolute for a medium with large enough nonlinearity. Fluctuations are then exposed to a transition to chaos. The transition procedure is complex but we conceive that the saturation of the nonlinear response can be effective. The latter is recognized by anomalous behavior of the optical system shown in Fig. 5.

There is still an ambiguity about the time delay incorporating in the energy transfer between the two graphene QDs. One can justify it by considering the ultrafast nonlinear response of graphene. From the other viewpoint, the formation of surface plasmon polaritons is due to the internal feedback mechanism. This will lead to the enhancement of the total feedback strength. However, this can be true for intraband transitions i.e. for the terahertz frequency range while our study has been restricted to the experiment that utilizes the visible light. Even so, another idea states that the nonlinear response in graphene is not merely explained neither by pure intraband nor by pure interband dynamics but their interplay should be considered [60].

In conclusion, a numerical solution of the NLSE based on PCM has shown that the chaotic behavior in a nonlinear medium containing RGO ink can be expressed by a hyper-chaotic map. This means that the procedure of route to chaos is more complicated than a typical nonlinear optical system in which the Ikeda instability is dominant. For example, through the latter, feedback mechanism is fulfilled by a delayed feedback circuit. However, we deduce that the instability is caused by energy transfer between the graphene QDs through the optical near-field interactions. These QDs are formed as a result of reduction process and are responsible for creation of small fluctuations which grow fast in amplitude due to increasing the interactions. This in turn, induces a saturation in the nonlinear response of the medium and subsequently initiates the absolute MI. In this study, we have brought an experimental proof to indicate that such transition to chaotic behavior is actually realized. Our simulation and experimental results and analyzes can be useful for developing the novel optical nanostructure based systems e.g. spasers and plasmonics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been funded and supported by Islamic Azad University, Parand branch, Parand, Tehran.
